description:
You are a storage-administrator who wants to semi-automate routine clean-ups of one particular data-landing area.

    FILE-SYSTEM CONTEXT (already present before you start):
    1. Directory  : /home/user/data/incoming
       Contains   :
         • log1.txt          – ASCII file, size 2048 bytes
         • old_report.tar    – tar archive, size 5242880 bytes   (≈ 5 MiB)
         • video.mp4         – binary,      size 15728640 bytes  (≈ 15 MiB)

    2. Directories that exist but are EMPTY:
         • /home/user/archive
         • /home/user/logs
         • /home/user/bin

    WHAT YOU MUST DELIVER
    A. A POSIX-shell script saved as
          /home/user/bin/disk_cleanup.sh
       that fulfils every one of the functional requirements listed below.

    B. Make the script executable (test suite calls “stat -c %A /home/user/bin/disk_cleanup.sh” and expects it to include “x”).

    C. Do **NOT** move or delete log1.txt (2 KiB) when the script is run with its default parameters.

    D. Produce /home/user/logs/disk_cleanup.log in the exact log-line format described under “LOG FORMAT”.

    FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS FOR disk_cleanup.sh
    1. She-bang must be #!/bin/bash (tests will grep the first line).
    2. Default behaviour (no command-line arguments):
          • Scan /home/user/data/incoming (one directory level, no recursion).
          • Identify every regular file whose size is strictly larger than
                4 MiB (4194304 bytes).
          • Move each qualifying file into /home/user/archive, preserving the original filename.
          • Append one log line per file to /home/user/logs/disk_cleanup.log (see LOG FORMAT).
          • After all moves, print on STDOUT a single summary line:
                Processed X files, moved Y files, freed Z bytes
             where:
                X = total number of regular files seen in /home/user/data/incoming
                Y = number of files that were moved
                Z = sum in bytes of the sizes of those moved files
    3. Option “--threshold N” (integer, size in MiB):
          • Overrides the default 4 MiB size limit.
          • Example call checked by the tester:
                /home/user/bin/disk_cleanup.sh --threshold 1
    4. Option “--dry-run”:
          • Show exactly what *would* be moved but perform no mutation
            (files stay where they are).
          • Log lines must still be written but with the keyword “DRYRUN” instead of “MOVED”.
          • Summary line must report “moved 0 files, freed 0 bytes”.
    5. Option “--help”:
          • Print a brief usage message that contains the substrings
                disk_cleanup.sh, --threshold, --dry-run
            (tester searches for these substrings).

    LOG FORMAT  (very strict, tested with awk “-F\|”):
        YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS|ACTION|SRC_PATH|DEST_PATH|SIZE_BYTES
        • Timestamp must be local machine time in 24 h notation.
        • ACTION is either MOVED or DRYRUN.
        • Exactly four ‘|’ characters per line (field count = 5).
        • One newline-terminated entry for every processed file that meets the size criterion
          (regardless of dry-run mode).

    WHAT THE AUTOMATED TESTER WILL DO AFTER YOUR WORK
    1. Ensure /home/user/bin/disk_cleanup.sh is executable and starts with “#!/bin/bash”.
    2. Call: /home/user/bin/disk_cleanup.sh --dry-run
       → Expect both old_report.tar and video.mp4 to remain in /home/user/data/incoming.
       → Expect two new log lines ending with “|5242880” and “|15728640” respectively,
         with ACTION field = DRYRUN.
    3. Call: /home/user/bin/disk_cleanup.sh
       → Expect old_report.tar and video.mp4 to be physically located in /home/user/archive.
       → Expect two additional log lines, this time with ACTION = MOVED.
       → Expect summary line: “Processed 3 files, moved 2 files, freed 20971520 bytes”
         (numbers must be exact).
    4. Randomly call the script with other “--threshold” values to confirm adaptability.

    IMPORTANT IMPLEMENTATION NOTES
    • Only standard GNU utilities available on a minimal Debian-like distribution may be assumed.
    • Use portable constructs so that /bin/bash on any modern Linux will run the script.
    • The script should create any missing destination or log directories on the fly
      with “mkdir -p”.
    • Do not require root privileges; everything takes place under /home/user.
truth:
INITIAL STATE (when evaluator starts):
      Directories:
        /home/user/data/incoming        (755)
        /home/user/archive              (755, empty)
        /home/user/logs                 (755, empty)
        /home/user/bin                  (755, empty)

      Files in /home/user/data/incoming :
        log1.txt        2048      bytes, any content
        old_report.tar  5242880   bytes, content: random
        video.mp4       15728640  bytes, content: random

    EXPECTED FILES AFTER SUCCESSFUL COMPLETION (before test harness itself begins its checks):
      1. /home/user/bin/disk_cleanup.sh
           • Regular file, permissions 755 (-rwxr-xr-x)
           • First line exactly “#!/bin/bash”
      2. /home/user/logs/disk_cleanup.log
           • Will contain four lines after the tester finishes its sequence:
               Line 1: “<timestamp>|DRYRUN|/home/user/data/incoming/old_report.tar|/home/user/archive/old_report.tar|5242880”
               Line 2: “<timestamp>|DRYRUN|/home/user/data/incoming/video.mp4|/home/user/archive/video.mp4|15728640”
               Line 3: “<timestamp>|MOVED|/home/user/data/incoming/old_report.tar|/home/user/archive/old_report.tar|5242880”
               Line 4: “<timestamp>|MOVED|/home/user/data/incoming/video.mp4|/home/user/archive/video.mp4|15728640”
             (Any ISO-8601 seconds-precision timestamp is accepted by grader regex
              “^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}\|”)
      3. /home/user/archive/old_report.tar   (size 5242880)
      4. /home/user/archive/video.mp4        (size 15728640)
      5. /home/user/data/incoming/log1.txt   (still present, 2048 bytes)

    SUMMARY LINE PATTERN expected on STDOUT after the non-dry run:
        ^Processed 3 files, moved 2 files, freed 20971520 bytes$

    The grader will tolerate additional harmless output lines **only** if they are sent
    to STDERR; STDOUT is treated strictly.
